1.exposition: The argument the whole letter has been building reaches its summit and its rest. The law was only a shadow of the good things to come, never their very form, and the writer marshals three converging proofs that its sacrifices could not perfect the worshipper: their endless repetition (vv.1–4), which betrayed that nothing final had been done; Christ's own word at his coming (vv.5–10), taking a body to do the will the offerings could not accomplish; and the posture he now holds (vv.11–14), seated where the priests must stand, because his one sacrifice is finished. Then the Holy Spirit seals the whole with Jeremiah's new-covenant promise (vv.15–18): where God remembers sin no more, there is no more offering for sin. Three separate grounds — the tabernacle's repetition, Psalm 40, and Jeremiah 31 — all converge on the one conclusion.
1.original_language: The section turns on a handful of decisive words. The law has a skia (“shadow, shade”), not the eikōn (“form, very image”) of the realities (v.1). At his coming the Son quotes the Septuagint of Psalm 40:6–8, which reads “a body (sōma) hast thou prepared me” where the Hebrew has “ears thou hast digged” — the opened ear of obedience and the prepared body of the obedient Servant meeting in one willing self-offering. The contrast of posture is sharpest of all: the priest “standeth” (hestēken) daily, but Christ “sat down” (ekathisen); and by one offering he has perfected — teteleiōken, a perfect tense of settled, abiding achievement — those who are sanctified (v.14).
1.historical_context: The addressees knew the sacrificial world from the inside, and the writer presses its own testimony against it. The “remembrance… of sins every year” (v.3) points to the Day of Atonement, whose annual return, with the high priest's threefold confession of sin, proclaimed that the guilt was never finally cancelled. The citation of Psalm 40 follows the Greek Bible the readers used: where the Hebrew speaks of an ear dug open for obedience, the Septuagint renders “a body you have prepared,” and the writer finds in that reading the incarnate body of the Son — the true and final offering.
1.christ: Every line here is Christology. The Son comes into the world to do the Father's will by the sacrifice of the body prepared for him (vv.5–10), and having offered it “one sacrifice for sins for ever,” he sat down on the right hand of God. The standing priests never sit, for their work is never done; the seated Christ sits because his is finished, accepted, and complete. “By one offering he hath perfected for ever them that are sanctified” (v.14) is the letter's central claim — the once-for-all sufficiency of the cross, not a limitation to be feared (“only once?”) but the guarantee that it truly worked (“once was enough”).

verses.note: The law is only a shadow of the good things to come, and not the very image of them. grammatical-historical Robertson presses the figures — skia, a mere shade, against eikōn, the solid form; puritan Matthew Henry sees the whole Levitical dispensation laid low the moment it is set beside Christ; wesleyan-arminian Wesley calls it a “bare, unsubstantial shadow,” not the substance. A shadow proves the object is coming but has none of its reality, so its sacrifices, offered “year by year continually,” could never make the comers perfect.

The proof of insufficiency is the endless repetition itself (v.2). grammatical-historical Robertson reads it as the conclusion of an unreal condition — had the sacrifices truly perfected, “would they not have ceased to be offered?” — and Ellicott calls the very repetition “a testimony to its imperfection.” A conscience once really purged would feel its guilt no more; the ceaseless renewing confessed that nothing final had been done.

Instead of removing sin, the annual rite made a remembrance again of sins every year. grammatical-historical Barnes fixes this on the great Day of Atonement, when the high priest's solemn confession recalled the nation's guilt. reformed Calvin grants that sins were remembered “that guilt might be removed” — yet only typically. The yearly reminder is the exact opposite of the covenant promise to remember sin no more (v.17).

The hinge of the whole argument, stated as a flat impossibility: it is not possible that the blood of bulls and of goats should take away sins. grammatical-historical Adam Clarke says common sense itself must have taught it — the death of beasts could neither satisfy justice nor cleanse the conscience. reformed Calvin keeps the sacrifices their due place: a symbol and pledge of the real cleansing to come in Christ, a shadow of the true offering.

“Wherefore when he cometh into the world, he saith… a body hast thou prepared me” — Psalm 40:6–8 placed in Christ's mouth at his incarnation. grammatical-historical Robertson notes the Septuagint reads “a body” where the Hebrew has “ears… digged”; the sense is one, for the opened ear of obedience and the prepared body of the obedient Servant both point to his willing self-offering. In place of the rejected animal offerings, God fits his Son a body to give.

The verdict is comprehensive: in burnt offerings and sacrifices for sin thou hast had no pleasure. grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the “whole burnt offering,” the symbol of entire consecration, is named among them — even the fullest sacrifice failed to please. Adam Clarke: God could never be satisfied by the legal victims as atonements, for they could neither honour his law nor answer his justice; his pleasure lay in the obedience of his Son.

“Then said I, Lo, I come… to do thy will, O God.” grammatical-historical Robertson marks the infinitive of purpose — the Messiah presents himself to accomplish the Father's will; wesleyan-arminian Wesley reads it plainly, “I come to do thy will — by the sacrifice of myself.” Where the animal offerings failed because they were not the obedience God sought, Christ comes in willing obedience, already written of him “in the volume of the book.”

The writer now expounds his own quotation (v.8). eastern Chrysostom hears it answer an objection: if the sacrifices were only types, why did they not cease when the truth came? — the Psalm foretold their setting aside. grammatical-historical Barnes notes the first half of the citation establishes the negative: God “wouldest not” the offerings “which are offered by the law,” so that Israel's own Scripture pronounces against them.

“Then said he, Lo, I come to do thy will… He taketh away the first, that he may establish the second.” grammatical-historical Adam Clarke identifies the two: the first is the legal offerings, the second “the offering of the body of Jesus once for all.” reformed Calvin: when the sacrifices are removed, the will of God stands as the perfect rule. The very Scripture the readers revered foretold the abolition of their own system.

The saving result: by the which will we are sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all. grammatical-historical Robertson notes the periphrastic perfect — “we have been sanctified,” a settled, accomplished state; Ellicott, that Christ did God's will in the sacrifice of obedience even unto death (Philippians 2:8). The many ineffectual offerings of the law are answered by the one effectual offering of Christ.

Every priest standeth daily ministering and offering oftentimes the same sacrifices, which can never take away sins. grammatical-historical Adam Clarke draws the contrast that governs the passage: the Jewish priest stands, his work never done; our High Priest sat down. reformed Calvin: daily sacrificing is wholly foreign to a priesthood that rests on one finished work. The unresting posture betrays the unfinished task.

“But this man, after he had offered one sacrifice for sins for ever, sat down on the right hand of God.” grammatical-historical Robertson makes “one sacrifice” the heart of it — the single act doing what the many never could; wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes he “sat down — as a Son, in majesty and honour,” not as a servant still labouring. The seated Christ declares the atonement finished and accepted.

From henceforth expecting till his enemies be made his footstool. grammatical-historical Robertson hears the settled attitude of certain victory, citing Psalm 110:1 and 1 Corinthians 15:25; Adam Clarke: he waits till all who oppose his priesthood are subdued. There is nothing more for him to do for sin; he waits only for the promised triumph.

The triumphant climax: by one offering he hath perfected for ever them that are sanctified. grammatical-historical Robertson stresses the perfect tense of teteleiōken — Christ “hath perfected,” a completed and abiding achievement, doing what the law “can never” do (v.1). The one offering has secured, once for all, the perfection the many sacrifices never could — full remission and consecration to holiness.

Whereof the Holy Ghost also is a witness to us. grammatical-historical Robertson notes the appeal to Jeremiah 31:33, already quoted at Hebrews 8:8, now to seal the argument; the prophet's words are the very testimony of the Spirit. wesleyan-arminian Wesley: here the apostle winds up his whole argument, the new covenant now fully ratified. The conclusion carries not the writer's authority only but God's own.

“This is the covenant… I will put my laws into their hearts, and in their minds will I write them.” grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the abridgment of the longer Jeremiah prophecy. Under the new covenant God writes his law not on tablets of stone, external and condemning, but within — so that obedience flows from an inward principle of grace, the fruit of the sanctification just described.

The crowning promise: and their sins and iniquities will I remember no more. grammatical-historical Gill puts it sharply — God's non-remembrance of sin is precisely the remission of sin; to be remembered no more is to be wholly forgiven. Where the yearly rite made “a remembrance… of sins every year” (v.3), the new covenant promises the opposite: full pardon replacing perpetual recall.

The logical and triumphant close: now where remission of these is, there is no more offering for sin. grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: where sin is once pardoned, no sin-offering can be needed; Ellicott notes the argument here reaches its climax, opening the exhortations that follow. A debt fully paid cannot be paid again — the whole rationale of the repeated sacrifices is not merely superseded but rendered needless.

witnesses.point: The types could not cease of themselves while the truth was absent; the Psalm itself foretold that Christ's coming would set the shadow-sacrifices aside.
The blood of beasts was a symbol and pledge of the real cleansing to come; when the sacrifices are removed, the will of God stands as the perfect rule of righteousness.
“One sacrifice” is the heart of the matter — the single offering doing what the many could not, crowned by the seated Christ and the perfect tense of teteleiōken, “he hath perfected.”
Christ came to do God's will “by the sacrifice of himself,” and sat down as a Son, in majesty — the new covenant now fully ratified.

external.name: Harold W. Attridge
external.point: Reads the Septuagint form of Psalm 40 as the linchpin of the argument: “a body you have prepared for me,” standing where the Hebrew has “ears,” turns the psalmist's language of obedience into a statement of the incarnation, so that the prepared body is itself the offering that supersedes the sacrifices.

1.application: God has said his last word about your sin, and it is “finished.” The seated Christ is the proof: nothing remains to be added to a sacrifice that has perfected forever those it sanctifies. Rest is not presumption here — it is faith taking God at his own word, “their sins… will I remember no more” (v.17).

19.exposition: With a single “therefore” the homily turns from what Christ has done to what his people must now do. Because the one sacrifice has opened the holiest and set a great High Priest over God's house (vv.19–21), three summons follow, each a volitive “let us”: draw near in full assurance, hold fast the confession of hope, and consider one another to stir up love and good works. The three move outward — from the believer before God, to the believer's confession before the world, to the community's mutual care — and land on a concrete charge: do not forsake the assembly, so much the more, as ye see the day approaching. The door stands open; the writer bids them walk through it, and walk through it together.
19.original_language: The way is described in two adjectives: prosphatos (“new,” literally “freshly made,” even “freshly slain”) and zōsa (“living”) — a way not merely newly opened but alive, because the risen Christ is himself the way (v.20). It runs “through the veil, that is to say, his flesh”: the katapetasma that screened the Holy of Holies is identified with his crucified body. The three exhortations are cohortative subjunctives — proserchōmetha (“let us draw near”), katechōmen (“let us hold fast”), katanoōmen (“let us consider”) — and the assembly not to be forsaken is the episynagōgē (v.25; cf. 2 Thessalonians 2:1).
19.historical_context: “Through the veil, that is to say, his flesh” (v.20) states theologically what the Gospels record as sign: at the crucifixion the temple veil was torn in two (Matthew 27:51; Mark 15:38; Luke 23:45), the barrier between the holy and the common opened by his death. The warning against “forsaking the assembling” (v.25) addresses a real pattern in the community — some were withdrawing from the gatherings, most likely from fear of being publicly identified as Christians under mounting pressure, and drifting back toward the safety of the synagogue.
19.christ: “A new and living way… through the veil, that is to say, his flesh” (v.20) compresses the whole gospel into a figure. The Aaronic way was old, annual, and passed through a curtain of cloth; Christ is the new (final), living (risen, permanent), way — the access itself, not merely a path to it. His body was the barrier, and his death was its tearing; the one who is “the way” (John 14:6) was rent like the veil to open the holiest to all who come through him. And he does not merely open the door but stands within it, an high priest over the house of God.

verses.note: Having therefore, brethren, boldness to enter into the holiest by the blood of Jesus. eastern Chrysostom notes the writer, having shown the difference of priest, sacrifice, tabernacle, and covenant, now draws the practical conclusion; grammatical-historical Robertson calls it the second great resume of the argument (Hebrews 4:14–16). The inmost sanctuary, entered under the law by one man once a year, now stands open to every believer — and the access rests wholly on the blood of Jesus.

By a new and living way, which he hath consecrated for us, through the veil, that is to say, his flesh. grammatical-historical Robertson glosses prosphaton, “freshly made,” and zōsan, “living” — a way none could tread before. reformed Calvin: the Godhead, hid in Christ's flesh, leads us into heaven. As the temple veil both hid and, torn, opened the Holy of Holies, so Christ's crucified flesh is the door — the very veil that screened God becomes, in its rending, the way in.

And having an high priest over the house of God. grammatical-historical Robertson renders it “a great priest,” set over God's house as a Son, not a servant like Moses (Hebrews 3:6); Adam Clarke takes the house as the church, over which Christ presides, offering his own blood and presenting his people's prayers. Beside the opened way stands the interceding Priest — the second pillar of the believer's confidence.

The first of three summons: let us draw near with a true heart in full assurance of faith. grammatical-historical Robertson notes proserchōmetha, the volitive subjunctive that governs the exhortation. The language is sacrificial — “hearts sprinkled from an evil conscience” by Christ's blood, “bodies washed with pure water,” perhaps with allusion to baptism. reformed Calvin: what we bring must answer to the spiritual nature of Christ's sacrifice — a cleansed heart and life.

Let us hold fast the profession of our faith without wavering; for he is faithful that promised. grammatical-historical Ellicott urges the better rendering, “our hope,” not “faith”; reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley relate it to the hope confessed at baptism. To a people under pressure to let go, the anchor is not their grip but God's fidelity: the confession need not waver, for it rests on a Promiser who cannot fail.

And let us consider one another to provoke unto love and to good works. grammatical-historical Robertson savours the word paroxysmos — our “paroxysm,” a stirring or spurring — here redeemed to a holy end: not provoking one another to anger but inciting to love. Adam Clarke: attentively considering each other's trials so as to excite mutual love and fruitfulness. The Christian life is not solitary; believers answer for one another's growth.

Not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together, as the manner of some is. grammatical-historical Robertson notes episynagōgē (2 Thessalonians 2:1) and the sober “as the manner of some is” — already some stayed away; Ellicott thinks they were drifting back toward the synagogue, wesleyan-arminian Wesley that they feared persecution or fancied themselves above ordinances. “So much the more, as ye see the day approaching” — the nearer the crisis, the more the gathering is needed. To forsake the assembly is the first step toward the apostasy the next verses warn against.

witnesses.point: Having shown the difference of priest, sacrifice, tabernacle, and covenant, he draws the practical conclusion: “let us draw near with a true heart in full assurance of faith.”
All the ceremonial barriers to God's presence are now removed; the Godhead hidden in Christ's flesh leads us into heaven, so that none finds God but through the man Christ.
The passage resumes the whole argument in three cohortative summons — draw near, hold fast, consider one another — on the double ground of the opened way and the great priest.

external.name: William L. Lane
external.point: Takes vv.19–25 as the pastoral hinge of the homily, where the entire priestly exposition is translated into three cohortative summons — draw near, hold fast, consider one another — addressed to the concrete life of a congregation tempted to drift from its confession.

19.application: The finished work of Christ is not meant to be admired from a distance but used. The same blood that opened the holiest bids you draw near — and it will not let you go alone: “consider one another,” and do not forsake the gathering where that mutual courage is kept alive (vv.24–25).

26.exposition: The open door has a dark lintel. Having summoned his readers to draw near and hold fast, the writer turns — as at every stage of the letter — to warn, and this fourth warning is the sharpest of the five, because it follows the fullest account of what Christ has done. If we sin wilfully after that we have received the knowledge of the truth names not the ordinary failures of the believer but deliberate, decisive apostasy — the renouncing of a Christ once known and professed. For such there remains “no more sacrifice for sins,” since they have cast away the only one; there is left a certain fearful looking for of judgment. An argument from the lesser to the greater follows (vv.28–29): if setting aside Moses' law brought death without mercy, how much sorer the doom of trampling the Son, profaning his blood, and outraging the Spirit. God himself has said, vengeance belongeth unto me — and it is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God.
26.original_language: “If we sin wilfully” renders hekousiōs hamartanontōn, a present participle of continued, deliberate action — a settled state of sinning, not a single lapse (v.26). The a fortiori argument turns on athetēsas, “having set at naught,” Moses' law (v.28). The apostate's guilt is drawn in three escalating verbs: he has “trodden under foot” (katapatēsas) the Son, “counted common” (koinon, the opposite of holy) the covenant-blood “wherewith he was sanctified,” and “done despite unto” (enybrisas, insulted, outraged) the Spirit of grace (v.29). The warning lands on phoberon — “a fearful thing” — to fall into the hands of the living God (v.31).
26.historical_context: The “wilful sin” echoes a distinction written into the law itself: for inadvertent, unintentional sins the Levitical system provided atonement (Leviticus 4:1–2), but for the “high-handed” or presumptuous sin the sinner was “cut off,” and no sacrifice was appointed (Numbers 15:30–31). The death penalty of v.28 is the sentence on apostasy and idolatry in Deuteronomy 17:2–7, carried out on the required witnesses. The two citations of v.30 are both drawn from the Song of Moses (Deuteronomy 32:35–36) — fitting law to confirm the warning to Jewish believers. The threefold description invokes the three Persons of the Trinity as the objects of the offense.
26.christ: The severity is measured by the dignity of what apostasy despises. To “tread under foot the Son of God” is to treat the incarnate, crucified, risen Lord as worthless; to count “the blood of the covenant… an unholy thing” is to reckon common the very blood that ratifies the new covenant and sanctifies; to “do despite unto the Spirit of grace” is to insult the Spirit whose whole work is grace (v.29). The warning is so grave precisely because the redemptive work spurned is so complete — the finished sacrifice just expounded turned into an object of contempt.

verses.note: If we sin wilfully after that we have received the knowledge of the truth, there remaineth no more sacrifice for sins. The witnesses define the sin narrowly. grammatical-historical Robertson reads the present participle as a settled state of wilful sin; puritan Matthew Henry, with Barnes, takes it as total, final apostasy — the deliberate renouncing of a known and professed Christ, not the ordinary sins of infirmity. The dreadful consequence: having cast away the one sacrifice, the apostate has no other to which he can turn.

What remains is a certain fearful looking for of judgment and fiery indignation, which shall devour the adversaries. grammatical-historical Ellicott sets the contrast: where Christ's waiting servants look for salvation (Hebrews 9:28), the apostate has only a terrified expectation of judgment. reformed Calvin adds the present torment of an evil conscience that knows it has tasted and lost grace by its own fault. By spurning the sacrifice he has made himself an “adversary,” and falls under the fire reserved for God's enemies.

He that despised Moses' law died without mercy under two or three witnesses. grammatical-historical Ellicott refers it to the death sentence on apostasy and idolatry in Deuteronomy 17:2–7, executed on the testimony required by Numbers 15:30. reformed Calvin names the form of the argument — from the less to the greater: if setting aside the law of Moses brought death without mercy, a heavier doom must await the one who rejects the gospel.

Of how much sorer punishment, the writer asks, is the apostate worthy who has “trodden under foot the Son of God,” counted “the blood of the covenant… an unholy thing,” and “done despite unto the Spirit of grace”? grammatical-historical Adam Clarke unfolds the threefold guilt against the three Persons: the Son trampled with contempt, his covenant-blood — “wherewith he was sanctified” — counted common and profane, the Spirit of grace insulted. wesleyan-arminian Wesley insists this describes only the wilful, total apostate; reformed Calvin, that the sorer punishment is not bodily death but eternal perdition. To spurn the Son, profane his blood, and insult the Spirit is the deepest of all impieties.

“For we know him that hath said, Vengeance belongeth unto me, I will recompense, saith the Lord. And again, The Lord shall judge his people.” grammatical-historical Robertson notes both citations come from the Song of Moses (Deuteronomy 32:35–36; cf. Romans 12:19). reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley press that “the Lord shall judge his people” means he will deal the more rigorously with those who, having known him, rebel — the nearness of the covenant aggravates the sin rather than excusing it.

It is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God. grammatical-historical Robertson refuses to soften it — “the sense is not to be explained away; the wrath of God faces wrongdoers”; Adam Clarke notes that he who lives for ever can punish for ever. To fall into God's hands for judgment, having spurned his mercy, is the most fearful of ends, for there is no escape from omnipotence and no end to eternity.

witnesses.point: Likens the illuminated who bear no fruit to trees which, for all the care spent on them, are “pulled up by the roots, and handed over to the fire.”
Insists the passage refers only to wilful, total apostasy — the deliberate “drawing back” from God — not to the ordinary sins of the believer.
The sorer punishment is not bodily death but eternal perdition; and “the Lord shall judge his people” means he deals the more rigorously with those who, having known him, rebel.
Unfolds the threefold contempt — the Son trampled, his covenant-blood counted profane, the Spirit of grace insulted — the deepest of all impieties.

external.name: Peter T. O'Brien
external.point: Reads the “deliberate sin” not as a moral lapse but as calculated, decisive apostasy — a public repudiation of Christ that parallels the “high-handed” sin of Numbers 15, for which the law itself provided no sacrifice; the warning is real and is addressed to the professing community.

26.application: A warning is an act of love, not its opposite. The writer holds the fire before his readers precisely to keep them from it — and the same passage that speaks most terribly of turning away speaks, by implication, most gloriously of holding fast: the blood that would be “profaned” by apostasy (v.29) is the blood that sanctifies all who draw near.

32.exposition: Like a wise physician who, having cut deep, now binds the wound gently, the writer turns from terror to encouragement. He bids his readers call to remembrance the former days — the season just after their conversion when, newly enlightened, they endured a hard contest of sufferings: publicly reproached, standing with the imprisoned, joyfully bearing the plunder of their goods because they knew they held a better and an enduring substance (vv.33–34). People who once endured so much must not now cast away their confidence; what they need is not a new beginning but endurance to the end (v.36). And the wait is short: yet a little while, and he that shall come will come. The chapter closes on Habakkuk's great principle — the just shall live by faith — and on the writer's confident hope: we are not of them who draw back unto perdition, but of them that believe.
32.original_language: “After ye were illuminated” translates phōtisthentes (v.32), a word that became an early Christian term for baptism; the “great fight of afflictions” is athlēsis, an athletic contest of suffering. In v.33 the readers were theatrizomenoi — “made a spectacle,” brought as on a stage before the public gaze. The grace now required is hypomonē, patient endurance (v.36). The closing citation conflates two texts: Isaiah 26:20's “yet a little while” introduces Habakkuk 2:3–4, and the writer's addition of the article makes “the Coming One” messianic. The opposite of faith is hypostellō, to “shrink back” or withdraw (vv.38–39).
32.historical_context: The “former days” recall a specific, remembered persecution soon after the community's conversion — public reproach, imprisonments, and the confiscation of property (vv.32–34), borne, remarkably, “with joy.” “Enlightened” (v.32) was used by the early church, as by Justin Martyr, as a synonym for baptism, so the phrase dates the sufferings to the period following their initiation. The scriptural ground in vv.37–38 joins Isaiah 26:20 to Habakkuk 2:3–4 — the very verse (“the just shall live by faith”) that Paul makes the theme of Romans 1:17 and Galatians 3:11.
32.christ: Habakkuk's “my righteous one shall live by faith” (v.38), set within its promise that “he that shall come will come, and will not tarry,” makes faith not merely good conduct but the posture of those who wait for the Lord's advent. It is the anchor text for the great gallery of faith that follows in chapter 11. The community that will not shrink back — that has stood with prisoners, borne the loss of its goods, and still keeps meeting — is even now enacting the faith of the ancestors, and doing it in the harder and more glorious light of the Son who has entered heaven on their behalf.

verses.note: Call to remembrance the former days, in which, after ye were illuminated, ye endured a great fight of afflictions. eastern Chrysostom likens the writer to the wise physician who, after a deep incision, soothes rather than cuts again; grammatical-historical Ellicott notes he turns from warning to encouragement, as in chapter 6. wesleyan-arminian Wesley glosses “illuminated” as enlightened with the knowledge of God. Their own past steadfastness is held up as both encouragement and gentle reproach.

Partly, whilst ye were made a gazingstock both by reproaches and afflictions; and partly, whilst ye became companions of them that were so used. grammatical-historical Robertson glosses theatrizomenoi — brought upon the stage to public derision (1 Corinthians 4:9). puritan Matthew Henry marks the two forms of their suffering: their own reproach, and the braver fellowship of standing with the persecuted when it was dangerous and shameful to do so.

“For ye had compassion of me in my bonds, and took joyfully the spoiling of your goods, knowing… that ye have in heaven a better and an enduring substance.” grammatical-historical Ellicott prefers the better text, “them that were in bonds” — the prisoners for the faith. wesleyan-arminian Wesley marks the secret of their cheerful loss: they knew they held a better, lasting possession, beside which the plunder of earthly goods was nothing. Heavenly hope made earthly loss a joy.

Cast not away therefore your confidence, which hath great recompence of reward. grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: do not throw away as worthless your liberty of access to God, your birthright as sons; reformed Calvin: confidence is the very foundation of a godly life. Having endured so much, the readers must not now, under fresh pressure, fling away the boldness their own endurance had proved.

For ye have need of patience, that, after ye have done the will of God, ye might receive the promise. grammatical-historical Robertson notes hypomonē, endurance — the grace the moment most requires; Adam Clarke holds the balance: God furnishes the grace, but the believer must exercise it. reformed Calvin: patience is needed the more because Satan harasses us with countless arts. Endurance is the bridge between the doing of God's will and the receiving of the promise.

For yet a little while, and he that shall come will come, and will not tarry. grammatical-historical Robertson hears in “a very little while” an echo of Isaiah 26:20 introducing Habakkuk 2:3, “he that cometh” given a messianic force. reformed Calvin: nothing so sustains the fainting mind as the hope of a near end to the conflict, as a general cheers his soldiers with the prospect of soon-coming victory. The reward is not far off.

Now the just shall live by faith: but if any man draw back, my soul shall have no pleasure in him. grammatical-historical Robertson notes the citation of Habakkuk 2:4, the same text Paul makes central (Romans 1:17). The righteous lives by faith — faith the principle and sustenance of his whole life; but the one who “draws back,” who shrinks and apostatizes, God abhors. wesleyan-arminian Wesley: he lives by faith so long as he retains that gift, but if he draws back, God casts him off. The two alternatives are set plainly before the wavering.

The chapter closes in confident hope: we are not of them who draw back unto perdition; but of them that believe to the saving of the soul. grammatical-historical Adam Clarke renders it, “we are not the cowards, but the courageous”; Barnes hears the writer's assurance that none of his readers would apostatize. Numbering himself with them on the better side, he sets the final alternative — drawing back to ruin, or believing to the saving of the soul — and leads straight into the catalogue of faith's heroes in chapter 11.

witnesses.point: Compares the writer to the best physicians, who after a deep incision do not cut again but soothe the wound with gentle remedies.
Nothing so sustains the fainting mind as the hope of a near end to the conflict; and it is shameful to begin well and faint midway, worse still to draw back.
God furnishes the grace of patience, but the believer must exercise it — here we are “workers together with God”; and the faithful are not cowards but the courageous.
The believer “lives by faith” so long as he retains that gift; but if he draws back, God has no pleasure in him — the two ways set plainly before the wavering.

external.name: F.F. Bruce
external.point: Observes that the writer prefaces Habakkuk's “the righteous shall live by faith” with words from Isaiah 26:20 (“yet a very little while”), and by adding the definite article gives “the Coming One” a messianic force the prophet's text did not carry — the apparent delay answered by the certainty of the One who comes.

32.application: Endurance is often just faith with a long horizon. The readers had already proved they could suffer and even rejoice in loss (v.34); the exhortation is not to become someone new but to keep on being who they had already shown they were. “Ye have need of patience” (v.36) — and the One you wait for “will not tarry.”

_meta.title: The One Sacrifice and the New and Living Way
reflection: The seated Christ “offered one sacrifice for sins for ever” and “perfected for ever them that are sanctified” (v.12, v.14). Where are you still trying to add to a work God already calls finished?

The writer warns against “forsaking the assembling of ourselves together” and pairs it with “exhorting one another” (v.25). What would change if you treated the gathering of God's people as essential to your endurance rather than optional to it?

“The just shall live by faith: but if any man draw back…” (v.38). The community had already borne reproach and the plundering of their goods with joy (v.34). What earlier season of costly faithfulness could you “call to remembrance” to strengthen you now?
